docs.title: Embr Voice
docs.text:
Voice dictation for any Windows app. Tap Ctrl + Win, speak, and Embr writes it into whatever you're typing into.

Free. Open source. Your own OpenAI key so audio and transcription never touch a third party.

docs.title: Get started in under a minute
docs.text:
1. Install Embr Voice (one download, no installer)
  2. Add your OpenAI key (takes 60 seconds, live mic check included)
  3. Do your first dictation (tap Ctrl + Win and talk)

docs.title: Already running?
docs.text:
• Hotkeys — the exact keys, tap vs hold, what to do when they conflict
  • Pause and resume — stop Embr listening without quitting
  • Settings — microphone source, language, start with Windows
  • Troubleshooting — no audio, wrong mic, API errors, hotkey conflicts
  • FAQ — privacy, pricing, what the API costs, how your audio is handled
  • Changelog — what shipped in every release

docs.title: Something broken?
docs.text:
• Ask in the tray menu → right-click Embr → Help → Ask Embr (built-in chatbot)
  • Open a discussion on GitHub
  • Report a bug with logs

docs.title: Why Embr exists
docs.text:
Typing is slow. Voice is not. Every major dictation tool is either paid-only, cloud-locked, or platform-locked to Mac. Embr is open source, Windows-native, and runs on your own API key so nothing is rented from a middleman.

If that resonates, star the repo and tell one other person.

docs.title: Your OpenAI API key
docs.text: Embr uses your own OpenAI key so audio and transcription never touch a third-party server. No accounts with Embr. No hidden middleman. Your key, your bill, your data.

docs.title: Get a key (60 seconds)
docs.text:
1. Go to platform.openai.com/api-keys
  2. Sign in (or create an OpenAI account — this is a separate account from ChatGPT.com but uses the same login)
  3. Click Create new secret key → give it a name like Embr Voice → copy the key (starts with sk-proj-)
  4. Back in Embr's onboarding, paste it into the input and hit Next

You only see the key once in the OpenAI dashboard, so paste it into Embr immediately. Embr stores it in Windows Credential Manager (encrypted per-user), not in any plain file.

docs.title: What will it cost me?
docs.text:
Embr uses OpenAI's audio transcription endpoint. At current pricing:

  • Whisper-1: $0.006 per minute of audio
  • GPT-4o-transcribe: $0.006 per minute of audio

A heavy user dictating 30 minutes per day = about $5.40/month. Most users land around $1–3/month.

You set your own spending limit in the OpenAI dashboard. Embr will show an error if the key hits its cap; it does not silently burn credit.

docs.title: Where is the key stored?
docs.text:
• Live key: Windows Credential Manager → generic credential Embr-OpenAI-Key (AES-encrypted with your Windows login)
  • Never written to: settings.json, any log file, or any network destination that isn't OpenAI's API

You can inspect this yourself. Open Control Panel → Credential Manager → Windows Credentials and find the Embr entry.

docs.title: Rotating or changing the key
docs.text:
• Change the key: tray icon → Settings → OpenAI key → Change
  • Revoke from OpenAI: delete the key in the OpenAI dashboard; Embr will show a clear "key rejected" error on next dictation
  • Clear from Windows: Credential Manager → delete the Embr-OpenAI-Key entry

docs.title: Using a company or team key
docs.text: Safe. Embr doesn't phone home or register the key anywhere. If you use a team key, only your own Windows profile has the credential; other profiles on the same machine can't read it.

docs.title: Offline / local transcription (coming in v1.3)
docs.text: v1.2 is cloud-only (OpenAI). v1.3 ships a bundled faster-whisper model as a local fallback — if your internet drops, Embr transcribes on-device. Slightly lower accuracy, zero cost, offline-friendly.

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: Human-readable history of what shipped in each Embr Voice release. Full commit log on GitHub.

docs.title: v1.2.0 — 2026-04 (in progress)
docs.text:
Reliability sprint. No new features, focused on making the first-run experience and core dictation loop rock-solid.

Added

  • 6-screen first-run onboarding wizard (Welcome / API key / Hotkey walkthrough / Try a dictation / Tray tour / Ready)
  • Live microphone level meter in onboarding and Settings
  • Microphone source selection (dropdown of all input devices)
  • Hotkey conflict warnings (detects Sticky Keys, Windows virtual-desktop shortcuts)
  • MkDocs Material handbook at billywellington.github.io/embr-voice/
  • In-app "Ask Embr" chatbot link (via tray menu → Help)

Fixed

  • Windows 11 taskbar icon drift across rebuilds (AppUserModelID + HKCU registration)
  • Onboarding dialog logo clipping at HiDPI (QPainter coord-space fix)
  • Tray icon missing orange accent on some Windows themes
  • Hotkey release detected before key-up on modifier combos
  • Silent dictation failure when OpenAI returns HTTP 5xx (now surfaces clear error toast)

Changed

  • Default hotkey mode is now tap-to-toggle (was hold-to-talk). Hold is opt-in via Settings.
  • Transcription model defaults to whisper-1 (was auto-select); users can switch to gpt-4o-transcribe in Settings.
  • Settings split into Dictation / Microphone / Appearance / Startup / Privacy / Advanced tabs.

Known issues

  • Windows 10 onboarding tray-tour illustration looks slightly off (Win 11 Mica/acrylic chrome mocked).
  • GPT-4o-transcribe can occasionally return punctuation-heavy transcriptions — workaround: switch to whisper-1 via Settings.

docs.title: v1.1.0 — 2026-03
docs.text:
Reliability sprint.

Added

  • Raw Input hook (replaces pynput low-level hook for lower CPU + no elevation needed)
  • Multi-monitor overlay positioning
  • Tray icon hide-fix (SWP_SHOWWINDOW flag diagnosis)

Fixed

  • Overlay didn't hide on quit in some cases
  • Mic stuck "holding" after hotkey release on fast taps

docs.title: v1.0.0 — 2026-02
docs.text:
First public release under the Embr Voice name (previously Kaira, previously Voxly).

Added

  • Windows tray app with hotkey-triggered dictation
  • OpenAI Whisper + GPT-4o-transcribe support
  • Translucent on-screen overlay while recording
  • Keyring storage for API key
  • Single-instance lock
  • Command Mode (press-and-hold for single-shot commands)

For commit-level detail, see the CHANGELOG.txt in the repo root.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: Short answers to the things people ask before they ask.

docs.title: Does my audio leave my computer?
docs.text: Yes, but only to OpenAI's API, and only while you're actively dictating. Your audio is sent to api.openai.com, transcribed, and the response comes back. Nothing is stored on an Embr-owned server (we don't have one).

docs.title: Does my audio stay with OpenAI?
docs.text: Per OpenAI's API data policy (as of 2026), audio sent to their API is not used to train their models and is retained for up to 30 days for abuse detection, then deleted. Read the latest at platform.openai.com/docs/models#data-usage.

docs.title: Can I use Embr fully offline?
docs.text: Not in v1.2 — v1.2 is cloud-only. v1.3 ships a bundled faster-whisper local model as a fallback. Lower accuracy, slower, but fully on-device and free.

docs.title: Does Embr have telemetry?
docs.text: No. v1.2 ships with zero telemetry. There is a disabled "Telemetry" toggle in Advanced settings reserved for v1.3+ opt-in anonymised stats (sessions, errors, model choice). It does nothing in v1.2.

docs.title: Where is my API key stored?
docs.text: Windows Credential Manager, AES-encrypted per user. Never in a plain text file. You can inspect it yourself via Control Panel → Credential Manager → Windows Credentials → Embr-OpenAI-Key.

docs.title: How much will the API cost me?
docs.text: OpenAI charges $0.006 per minute of audio. A heavy user at 30 min/day = about $5.40/month. Most users land at $1–3/month. Set a spending cap in your OpenAI dashboard for peace of mind.

docs.title: Does Embr charge a monthly fee?
docs.text: No. Embr is free and open source. We don't have a subscription, a Pro tier, or a paywall. v1.3 may introduce optional paid conveniences (managed API billing, cloud sync) — the free tier stays free forever.

docs.title: Can I use a free OpenAI trial credit?
docs.text: Yes. If your OpenAI account has trial credit, Embr uses it transparently. When the credit runs out you'll get an HTTP 429 / 402 and Embr will tell you to top up.

docs.title: Does Embr work on Windows 10?
docs.text: Partial support. Tested on Windows 10 22H2 — the tray icon, hotkey, dictation all work. The onboarding's Win 11–style tray tour looks slightly off. If something specific breaks on Win 10, file an issue.

docs.title: Does Embr work on Mac or Linux?
docs.text: Not yet. v1.2 is Windows-only. Mac support is planned for v1.5 (Q3 2026 estimate). Linux is community-maintained if someone steps up.

docs.title: Does Embr work on ARM / Copilot+ PCs?
docs.text: Yes. PyQt6 runs on ARM64 Windows. The bundled .exe is x64; an ARM64 native build ships with v1.3.

docs.title: Does Embr work inside Microsoft Remote Desktop / Citrix / VMware VM?
docs.text: Mixed. Audio capture inside remote sessions is often blocked by the host. Embr will start but won't get mic data. This is a platform limitation, not an Embr bug.

docs.title: Can I dictate in languages other than English?
docs.text: Yes. Whisper supports 99 languages. Set the language explicitly in Settings → Dictation → Language for better accuracy than auto-detect.

docs.title: Can I dictate code?
docs.text: It works but unevenly. Whisper transcribes "open paren" as ( some of the time but not reliably. Use a code-aware model (GPT-4o-transcribe) and speak punctuation explicitly if you do this often.

docs.title: Can I customise the hotkey?
docs.text: Yes. Settings → Dictation → Change hotkey. Must include at least one modifier (Ctrl, Alt, Win, Shift). See Hotkeys.

docs.title: Can I pause Embr temporarily?
docs.text: Yes. Right-click the tray icon → Pause. See Pause and resume.

docs.title: Can Embr auto-pause during screen-shares?
docs.text: Yes. Settings → Privacy → Pause when screen-sharing detected. Off by default.

docs.title: Why is the taskbar icon showing a generic icon instead of the orange waveform?
docs.text:
Windows 11 resolves taskbar icons via AppUserModelID. If the icon is wrong:

  1. Close all Embr windows (quit from tray).
  2. Run ie4uinit.exe -show from a regular PowerShell.
  3. Restart explorer.exe (Task Manager → Details → explorer.exe → End task → File → Run new task → explorer.exe).
  4. Relaunch Embr.

Still wrong? File an issue with your Windows version (winver) and a screenshot.

docs.title: Can I contribute?
docs.text: Yes. Embr is open source. See CONTRIBUTING.md in the repo. Bugs, docs improvements, translations, and small features are all welcome.

docs.title: Can I fork Embr?
docs.text: Yes. MIT license. Fork freely. If you ship a commercial derivative, keep the MIT notice.

docs.title: How do I request a feature?
docs.text: GitHub Discussions → Ideas. Search first — someone may already have.

docs.title: Who builds Embr?
docs.text: Embr Voice is maintained by Billy Wellington at Intellium Inc., based in Cape Town, South Africa. Currently a solo project with community contributors.

docs.title: Is Embr a company or a product?
docs.text: A free open-source product, built alongside Intellium's paid services (websites, automation, consulting). There is no "Embr Inc." — the brand is a marker for the tool itself.

docs.title: What happens if the maintainer disappears?
docs.text: MIT-licensed source is in the GitHub repo. The community can fork, or a new maintainer can take over. Your locally-installed copy keeps working forever; it doesn't phone home, doesn't license-check, doesn't expire.

docs.title: The one-line version
docs.text: Click into any text field in any Windows app. Tap Ctrl + Win. Speak. Tap Ctrl + Win again to stop. Your words appear typed into the field.

docs.title: Step-by-step
docs.text:
1. Make sure Embr is running. Look for the orange waveform mark in your system tray (bottom-right of the taskbar, possibly hidden behind the ⌄ chevron). If you don't see it, double-click Embr.exe again.
  2. Click where you want to dictate. A browser search box. A Slack message. A Notepad window. Anywhere that accepts typed text.
  3. Tap Ctrl + Win once. The tray icon pulses to tell you Embr is listening. A small subtle overlay may appear to indicate recording.
  4. Speak naturally. Don't over-enunciate. Don't pause awkwardly. Punctuate if you want ("…new paragraph, then period").
  5. Tap Ctrl + Win again to stop. Embr sends your audio to OpenAI, waits a second or two, then types the transcription into wherever your cursor was.

docs.title: What you'll notice the first time
docs.text:
• Latency: The first dictation is usually the slowest because OpenAI's API is "cold". Expect 1–3 seconds. Subsequent dictations are faster (often under 1 second for short utterances).
  • Punctuation: Whisper handles ". ? !" automatically from intonation. If it's missing, say it out loud — "new line", "new paragraph", "comma", "period".
  • Corrections: If it mis-hears you, just dictate the correction or type it. Embr doesn't rewrite; it inserts where the cursor is.

docs.title: Tap vs. hold
docs.text:
Ctrl + Win defaults to TAP-to-toggle:

  • Tap once → start listening
  • Tap again → stop and transcribe

If you prefer HOLD-to-talk (like walkie-talkie):

  • Open Settings → Dictation → set Hotkey mode to Hold
  • Now: press-and-hold Ctrl + Win while you speak, release to transcribe

Tap is the default because most users prefer not to hold a key for a long dictation. Hold is better for short bursts.

docs.title: Where it doesn't work
docs.text:
• Elevated / admin windows — Windows security forbids a non-elevated app from sending keystrokes to admin windows. If you need Embr to type into an elevated window (Task Manager, Registry Editor), right-click Embr.exe → Run as administrator.
  • Password fields — by design. Embr detects password fields and skips them.
  • Some games / DirectInput apps — Embr uses SendInput which bypasses most games but some anti-cheat-protected games block injected keystrokes.

docs.title: What if nothing happens when I tap Ctrl + Win?
docs.text: See Troubleshooting → Hotkey not responding.

docs.title: What if it hears me wrong?
docs.text: Usually a microphone issue, not a transcription issue. See Troubleshooting → Mic problems.

docs.title: Default
docs.text: Action Keys Start / stop dictation (tap mode) Ctrl + Win Start / stop dictation (hold mode, opt-in) Hold Ctrl + Win while speaking Show Embr overlay Ctrl + Shift + Win Cancel current recording Esc while recording

docs.title: Tap vs Hold — which is right for you?
docs.text:
• Tap (default): best for multi-sentence dictations, emails, long thoughts. One tap to start, another to stop. Your hands are free while speaking.
  • Hold: best for short bursts, quick replies, Slack messages. Press while speaking, release when done.

Change in Settings → Dictation → Hotkey mode.

docs.title: Why Ctrl + Win?
docs.text:
• Globally unused by Windows defaults (no conflict with Win + letter shortcuts, no conflict with Ctrl + letter shortcuts in most apps).
  • Two hands — hard to trigger accidentally.
  • Mid-row — comfortable to reach without looking.

docs.title: Known conflicts
docs.text:
Windows reserves a few Ctrl + Win combinations for its own use:

Conflict What it does Embr behaviour Ctrl + Win + D Create virtual desktop Ignored by Embr (won't trigger dictation) Ctrl + Win + ←/→ Switch virtual desktops Ignored Ctrl + Win + F4 Close virtual desktop Ignored

So: Ctrl + Win alone (no extra key) is yours. Combos that add a third key go to Windows.

docs.title: Changing the hotkey
docs.text:
Settings → Dictation → Change hotkey. You'll see a "Press the new combo…" prompt.

Rules:

  • Must include Ctrl, Alt, Win, or Shift as at least one modifier
  • Cannot be a single key
  • Cannot be F1 (Windows Help) or PrtScn (Screenshot)
  • Embr will warn if your chosen combo conflicts with a known Windows shortcut

Common alternatives users pick:

  • Alt + Space — very fast, but conflicts with window menu in some apps
  • Ctrl + ` — under the tilde, easy reach
  • Win + / — clean and uncontested

docs.title: Sticky keys interference
docs.text:
If you have Sticky Keys enabled in Windows Ease of Access, pressing Ctrl twice or holding Ctrl + Win may trigger the Sticky Keys prompt ("Do you want to turn on Sticky Keys?"). Embr shows a one-time advisory on boot if Sticky Keys is detected.

To disable Sticky Keys: Settings → Accessibility → Keyboard → Sticky keys → off.

docs.title: Reset to default
docs.text: Settings → Dictation → Reset hotkey restores Ctrl + Win.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: Embr Voice is a single .exe for Windows 11. No installer, no admin rights, no DLLs to unpack.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Windows 11 (Windows 10 22H2 works but is not primary-tested)
  • A working microphone (built-in, USB, or Bluetooth)
  • An OpenAI API key (get one)
  • About 400 MB of disk space (mostly the bundled transcription model)

docs.title: Download
docs.text:
Go to the latest release and grab Embr-v1.2.0.zip.

Unzip anywhere. Double-click Embr.exe inside the extracted Embr/ folder.

Put it somewhere permanent

Don't unzip into Downloads/ and leave it there — when you empty Downloads, Embr goes with it. A common spot is C:\Users\<you>\Tools\Embr\.

docs.title: First-run wizard
docs.text:
On first launch Embr opens a 6-step wizard:

  1. Welcome — tells you what Embr does
  2. Paste your OpenAI API key — saved to Windows Credential Manager (not a plain file)
  3. Hotkey walkthrough — shows you Ctrl + Win as the dictation trigger
  4. Try a dictation — speaks into a real textarea with your real mic
  5. Tray tour — shows where Embr lives in your Windows taskbar
  6. You're ready — tap Ctrl + Win anywhere in Windows

You can skip any screen. The wizard only runs once — after that Embr launches silent-to-tray.

docs.title: Start with Windows
docs.text: Open the tray icon → Settings → Start with Windows toggle. This adds a lightweight launcher to your startup folder (no scheduled task, no service). Uncheck at any time.

docs.title: Uninstall
docs.text:
Embr doesn't write to the registry, ProgramFiles, or anywhere else outside its own folder (with two exceptions, see below). To uninstall:

  1. Right-click the tray icon → Quit
  2. Delete the Embr/ folder
  3. Optional cleanup:
    • %APPDATA%\Embr\ — settings.json and logs
    • Windows Credential Manager → generic credential named Embr-OpenAI-Key
    • HKCU\Software\Classes\AppUserModelId\Intellium.EmbrVoice.Dictation.3 (taskbar icon association)

docs.title: Smartscreen or Defender warning
docs.text: Because Embr is new and unsigned, Windows SmartScreen may show "Windows protected your PC" the first time you run it. Click More info → Run anyway. This will go away once Embr accumulates enough reputation (or once we sign the build, which is planned for v1.3).

docs.title: In plain English
docs.text:
• Use Embr for anything you want, including commercial use.
  • Modify it, fork it, redistribute it.
  • Bundle it into another product as long as you keep the MIT notice.
  • We make no warranty — if it breaks or loses your work, that's on you.

docs.title: Third-party notices
docs.text:
Embr Voice depends on other open-source projects:

Project License PyQt6 GPL v3 (Riverbank commercial license for closed-source use) openai-python Apache 2.0 sounddevice MIT silero-vad-lite MIT ctranslate2 / faster-whisper MIT keyring MIT pynput LGPL

Full third-party license text is bundled with the .exe at _internal/THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.txt.

docs.title: Trademarks
docs.text:
"Embr Voice" and the Embr waveform mark are trademarks of Intellium Inc. The MIT license does not grant trademark rights — forks that significantly diverge from the project should use a different name.

"OpenAI", "Whisper", and "GPT-4" are trademarks of OpenAI. Embr Voice is not affiliated with OpenAI.

"Windows" is a trademark of Microsoft. Embr Voice is not affiliated with Microsoft.

docs.title: Pause and resume
docs.text: Sometimes you want Embr to stop listening for the hotkey temporarily — during a meeting, while playing a game, during a screen-share, when a colleague is borrowing your machine.

docs.title: Pause (temporarily stop responding to the hotkey)
docs.text:
Right-click the Embr tray icon → Pause.

While paused:

  • Tapping Ctrl + Win does nothing (Embr ignores it)
  • The tray icon turns grey so you know at a glance
  • Embr stays running, keeps your API key loaded, keeps your settings in memory — it just doesn't listen

To resume: right-click tray icon → Resume (same menu item toggles).

docs.title: Pause automatically in specific apps
docs.text:
Settings → Dictation → Auto-pause in apps lets you list app executables where Embr should auto-pause:

  • obs.exe (streaming / recording)
  • notion.exe (already has voice)
  • gameapp.exe (to prevent accidental dictation)

Type the .exe name. Embr watches the foreground window and pauses/resumes automatically.

docs.title: Pause during screen-share (auto-detect)
docs.text: Settings → Privacy → Pause when screen-sharing detected. Embr detects when Teams, Zoom, Meet, or Discord is actively sharing your screen and auto-pauses. Unchecked by default.

docs.title: Quit Embr completely
docs.text: Right-click tray icon → Quit. Removes the tray icon and terminates the process. Your API key stays in Credential Manager; relaunching Embr.exe picks up right where you left off.

docs.title: Why pause instead of just quit?
docs.text: Starting Embr fresh reloads the transcription model (about 1–2 seconds). Pause keeps everything warm — resume is instant. For a 10-minute break, pause. For overnight, quit.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
Open via: tray icon → Settings.

All settings persist to %APPDATA%\Embr\settings.json.

docs.title: Dictation
docs.text: Setting Default Notes Hotkey Ctrl + Win Change via Settings → Dictation → Change hotkey Hotkey mode Tap Tap toggles, Hold is press-and-hold-while-speaking Language Auto-detect Explicit language (English, Afrikaans, French, etc.) is more accurate Transcription model whisper-1 Alternative: gpt-4o-transcribe (slightly better on accents, same price) Auto-pause in apps empty Comma-separated .exe names where Embr won't respond

docs.title: Microphone
docs.text:
Setting Default Notes Input device System default Drop-down of all input devices detected on your system Input gain 1.0× Bump to 1.5× or 2× if your mic is quiet Noise suppression On Uses Silero VAD to trim silence and background noise before sending to OpenAI Mic test Button Opens a live level meter — speak and watch the bars move

If the bars don't move when you speak, you have the wrong input device selected.

docs.title: Appearance
docs.text: Setting Default Notes Overlay visibility Subtle Tiny orange waveform pill while recording. Options: off / subtle / prominent Overlay position Bottom-right Corner of the screen where the recording indicator appears Tray icon Colour Alternative: Monochrome for minimal Windows themes

docs.title: Startup
docs.text: Setting Default Notes Start with Windows Off Writes a shortcut to shell:startup; no scheduled task, no service Start minimised to tray On No window flashes on startup; Embr goes straight to tray Check for updates On Once per day, checks GitHub Releases — does not auto-download

docs.title: Privacy
docs.text: Setting Default Notes Pause when screen-sharing detected Off Detects Teams / Zoom / Meet / Discord active share Save transcription history Off If on, the last 100 dictations are saved to %APPDATA%\Embr\history.json (never uploaded) Telemetry Off Embr ships with zero telemetry. Ever. This toggle is reserved for v1.3+ opt-in anonymised usage stats; v1.2 does nothing

docs.title: Advanced
docs.text: Setting Default Notes API endpoint https://api.openai.com/v1 Change to a self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoint (e.g. Azure OpenAI, Ollama with whisper-server) HTTP timeout 30s Raise if you're on a slow connection Log level Info Debug is verbose; use when filing a bug Open logs folder Button Opens %APPDATA%\Embr\logs\ in Explorer Reset all settings Button Clears settings.json and runs the first-run wizard again (keeps your API key)

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Most issues fall into one of six buckets. Find the symptom, try the fix, escalate only if it's still broken.

docs.title: Hotkey not responding
docs.text:
Symptom: You tap Ctrl + Win, nothing happens. No tray pulse, no overlay.

  • Check Embr is actually running: look for the orange waveform in the tray. If you don't see it (even after expanding the ⌄ chevron), Embr isn't running. Double-click Embr.exe again.
  • Check Embr isn't paused: tray icon is grey when paused. Right-click → Resume.
  • Check the hotkey wasn't changed: Settings → Dictation → Hotkey. Default is Ctrl + Win.
  • Admin windows refuse keystrokes from non-admin apps. If you're trying to dictate into Task Manager / Registry Editor / an elevated PowerShell, right-click Embr.exe → Run as administrator.
  • Gaming mode may be intercepting: Xbox Game Bar, Razer Cortex, NVIDIA GeForce Overlay all hook Win combos aggressively. Disable them for a quick test.
  • Sticky Keys prompt: if you see a "Do you want to turn on Sticky Keys?" dialog when pressing Ctrl + Win, disable Sticky Keys. Settings → Accessibility → Keyboard → Sticky keys → off.

docs.title: Microphone
docs.text:
Symptom: Embr records but the transcription comes back empty, or always wrong.

  • Open Settings → Microphone → Mic test. Speak and watch the bars. If they don't move, the wrong input device is selected — change the dropdown.
  • Check Windows itself hears you: Settings → System → Sound → Input. Speak. Windows should show the input level moving. If it doesn't, it's a Windows-level issue, not Embr.
  • USB / Bluetooth mic just plugged in: Windows sometimes silently falls back to the laptop mic. Reselect your USB/Bluetooth device in Embr's Settings.
  • Bluetooth headset in HSP mode: HSP (low-quality call mode) has bad audio. Pair in A2DP for music / Handsfree profile for dictation — consult your headset's docs.
  • Input gain too low: Settings → Microphone → Input gain → bump to 1.5× or 2×.
  • Background noise: if Embr transcribes the TV/radio/aircon, enable Noise suppression (Settings → Microphone → Noise suppression).

docs.title: API errors
docs.text: Symptom: Embr listens but shows an error toast when it tries to transcribe.

docs.title: "API key rejected" / HTTP 401
docs.text:
• Your OpenAI key is wrong, revoked, or hit its spending cap.
  • Go to platform.openai.com/api-keys → verify it still exists.
  • Check billing at platform.openai.com/settings/organization/billing.
  • Regenerate a new key and paste into Embr: Settings → OpenAI key → Change.

docs.title: "Rate limited" / HTTP 429
docs.text:
• You've hit OpenAI's rate limit (unusual for personal use).
  • Wait 60 seconds.
  • If it keeps happening, check platform.openai.com/settings/organization/limits.

docs.title: "Network error" / HTTP 0 / timeout
docs.text:
• Check you're online.
  • Check a VPN isn't intercepting api.openai.com.
  • Check Windows Firewall hasn't blocked Embr.exe.
  • Settings → Advanced → HTTP timeout → bump to 60s if you're on slow internet.

docs.title: "Service unavailable" / HTTP 5xx
docs.text:
• OpenAI is having an outage. Check status.openai.com.
  • v1.3 adds a local faster-whisper fallback that transcribes on your machine when OpenAI is down.

docs.title: Transcription quality
docs.text:
Symptom: It hears you but the words are wrong.

  • Set an explicit language (Settings → Dictation → Language). "Auto-detect" guesses poorly for accented English and bilingual speakers.
  • Try gpt-4o-transcribe (Settings → Dictation → Model). Same price as whisper-1, often better on accents and fast speech.
  • Speak normally. Don't shout, don't over-enunciate, don't pause between words. Whisper is trained on natural speech.
  • Short noise-filled recordings are hardest. Keep dictations over 2 seconds; under 1 second often fails.

docs.title: Typed text goes to the wrong place
docs.text:
Symptom: Embr transcribes correctly but types into the wrong app / wrong field.

  • Your cursor was somewhere else when you stopped recording. Embr types into whatever has focus at the moment transcription returns, not the moment you started.
  • Don't click elsewhere during dictation. Start the dictation, keep your cursor in the target field, stop the dictation.
  • Password fields are skipped by design. If you're trying to dictate into a password field (not recommended), Embr refuses.

docs.title: Tray icon missing / wrong
docs.text:
Symptom: Embr is running (you can see it in Task Manager) but no tray icon, or the tray shows the generic Python icon instead of the orange waveform.

  • Icon is hidden in the chevron flyout. Click the ⌄ at the right end of the taskbar — Embr is often tucked inside. Drag it back to the visible area if you use it often.
  • Old Windows icon cache: restart explorer.exe. Open Task Manager → Details → explorer.exe → End task → File → Run new task → explorer.exe.
  • Wrong icon specifically: see FAQ → Why is the taskbar icon showing a generic icon.

docs.title: Still stuck?
docs.text:
1. Reproduce the issue once with Debug logging on. Settings → Advanced → Log level → Debug. Try again. Log level → Info.
  2. Open Settings → Advanced → Open logs folder. Grab the most recent .log file.
  3. File an issue at github.com/billywellington/embr-voice/issues/new and drop the log file in.

Or ask the in-app chatbot: tray icon → Help → Ask Embr. It's trained on this handbook and can answer the common stuff without you leaving the app.
